summary: Tractate Yoma daf 81b covers: Yom Kippur itself is called 'Shabbat' by the Torah; implications for the work prohibition; Food not fit for human consumption does not count toward the measure; bile and other unfit foods.

display.whats: Yom Kippur itself is called 'Shabbat' by the Torah; implications for the work prohibition
display.hint: The Torah calls Yom Kippur 'Shabbat Shabbaton' - a Shabbat of complete rest. The Gemara explores what this means for the structure of YK's work prohibition.

learning.summary: The Torah calls Yom Kippur 'Shabbat Shabbaton' - a Shabbat of complete rest. The Gemara explores what this means for the structure of YK's work prohibition.
learning.ahaMoment: YK is 'Shabbat Shabbaton': the emphatic doubling makes YK even more solemn than a regular Shabbat, hence the comparison of their labor rules.
learning.memoryAnchor: Shabbat Shabbaton: double Shabbat, double gravity - YK's work prohibition derives from its name.
learning.openQuestion: What is the deeper significance of the ruling about yom kippur itself is called 'shabbat' by the torah; implicat?

argumentFlow.label: Yom Kippur is itself called 'Shabbat'
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Rav Pappa derives the warning for affliction from the fact that Yom Kippur is itself called Shabbat - 'from evening until evening you shall rest on your Shabbat' (Leviticus 23:32). A verse about the matter itself is stronger than a verbal analogy, which is why Rav Pappa prefers it over Rav Acha bar Yaakov's gezerah shavah.

argumentFlow.label: Why Rav Acha did not use Rav Pappa's verse
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Rav Acha bar Yaakov needs 'keep your Shabbat' for a separate baraita teaching the obligation to add time before Yom Kippur: 'the ninth day at evening' shows one begins affliction while it is still day on the ninth, not at nightfall.

argumentFlow.label: Adding time at both ends and to Festivals
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: 'From evening until evening' teaches that one adds time at the end of Yom Kippur just as at the beginning, and 'you shall rest' extends the duty to add sanctified time before and after Festivals as well.

argumentFlow.label: 'On the ninth' for the tanna of the gezerah shavah
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The tanna who uses the 'same day' analogy reads 'afflict on the ninth' per Chiyya bar Rav of Difti: since one actually fasts on the tenth, the verse teaches that whoever eats and drinks on the ninth is credited as if he fasted on both the ninth and the tenth.

theological: YK is 'Shabbat Shabbaton': the emphatic doubling makes YK even more solemn than
narrative: Yom Kippur itself is called 'Shabbat' by the Torah; implicat

requiresUnderstanding: Background knowledge of Shabbat Shabbaton context
Understanding of Yom Kippur halachic framework

misconceptions.misconception: The verse 'afflict on the ninth' means the fast literally begins on the ninth of Tishrei.
misconceptions.correction: The fast is on the tenth; the ninth's mention teaches the obligation to add time before Yom Kippur and credits eating on the ninth as part of the mitzva.

visualizableElements.name: Yom Kippur itself is called 'Shabbat' by the Torah

quizSeeds.question: Why does Rav Pappa prefer deriving the affliction warning from Yom Kippur being called 'Shabbat' rather than a verbal analogy?
quizSeeds.answer: A verse that speaks about the matter itself is stronger than a gezerah shavah, so the direct designation of Yom Kippur as 'Shabbat' is the preferred source.

quizSeeds.question: What does Chiyya bar Rav of Difti learn from 'afflict on the ninth' when the fast is on the tenth?
quizSeeds.answer: One who eats and drinks on the ninth in preparation for the fast is credited as though he afflicted himself on both the ninth and the tenth.

sugyot.finalRuling: The Torah calls Yom Kippur 'Shabbat Shabbaton' - a Shabbat of complete rest. The Gemara explores what this means for the structure of YK's work prohib

display.whats: Food not fit for human consumption does not count toward the measure; bile and other unfit foods
display.hint: Food that is not fit for human consumption does not count toward the YK eating measure. The Gemara discusses bile, bitter substances, and other unpalatable foods.

learning.summary: Food that is not fit for human consumption does not count toward the YK eating measure. The Gemara discusses bile, bitter substances, and other unpalatable foods.
learning.ahaMoment: Unfit food doesn't count: the eating prohibition is about eating as a pleasure - something you cannot eat is not a pleasure, and withholding it is not an affliction.
learning.memoryAnchor: Bile is not eating: unfit food fails the pleasure test and doesn't count toward the measure.
learning.openQuestion: What is the deeper significance of the ruling about food not fit for human consumption does not count toward the?

argumentFlow.label: Unfit food does not count: Rava on pepper and ginger
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The Mishnah exempts one who eats food unfit for eating. Rava rules that chewing raw pepper or raw ginger on Yom Kippur carries no liability, since that is not the normal way of eating.

argumentFlow.label: Objection from Rabbi Meir on orla
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Rabbi Meir, reading the orla verse, includes a tree 'whose wood is eaten' - identified as pepper - implying pepper is in fact a food, which seems to contradict Rava.

argumentFlow.label: Resolving pepper: fresh versus dry
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The contradiction dissolves: Rabbi Meir speaks of fresh, moist pepper, which is food; Rava's exemption speaks of dry pepper, which is not considered food.

argumentFlow.label: Resolving ginger: wet versus dry
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Ravina presses the same with ginger, which Rav Nachman called edible and worthy of a blessing. Same answer: wet ginger is food, dry ginger is not, so the Yom Kippur exemption stands for the dry form.

argumentFlow.label: Reed leaves and grapevine shoots
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: A baraita exempts eating reed leaves but holds one liable for grapevine shoots. Rabbi Yitzchak of Migdal counts shoots that sprouted between Rosh HaShana and Yom Kippur as food; Rav Kahana counts those up to thirty days before.

argumentFlow.label: Brine implies vinegar is a beverage
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The Mishnah exempts drinking fish brine, implying one is liable for vinegar. This follows Rabbi Yehuda HaNasi, who held that vinegar revives the spirit and so counts as a beverage.

argumentFlow.label: Rav Giddel's ruling misapplied
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Rav Giddel taught publicly that the halacha is not like Rabbi Yehuda HaNasi, so vinegar is not a beverage. The next year people drank diluted vinegar on Yom Kippur; he protested that he had only said one is not liable after the fact, never that drinking it is permitted to begin with.

theological: Unfit food doesn't count: the eating prohibition is about eating as a pleasure -
narrative: Food not fit for human consumption does not count toward the

requiresUnderstanding: Background knowledge of food fitness context
Understanding of Yom Kippur halachic framework

misconceptions.misconception: If a substance is exempt from Yom Kippur liability, it is permitted to consume it.
misconceptions.correction: Exemption from punishment is not permission: Rav Giddel's case shows that vinegar may incur no after-the-fact liability yet still be forbidden to drink ab initio.

visualizableElements.name: Food not fit for human consumption does not count

quizSeeds.question: How does the Gemara resolve the contradiction between Rava and Rabbi Meir about pepper?
quizSeeds.answer: Rabbi Meir speaks of fresh, moist pepper, which counts as food, while Rava's Yom Kippur exemption refers to dry pepper, which is not considered food.

quizSeeds.question: What did Rav Giddel actually mean when he ruled against Rabbi Yehuda HaNasi on vinegar?
quizSeeds.answer: Only that one incurs no liability after the fact for drinking vinegar - not that drinking it on Yom Kippur is permitted in the first place.

sugyot.finalRuling: Food that is not fit for human consumption does not count toward the YK eating measure. The Gemara discusses bile, bitter substances, and other unpala

rashiTranslations.he: עינוי. על דבר אשר ענה את אשת רעהו (דברים כב) : היא גופה
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi: Yom Kippur is called 'Shabbat Shabbaton' in the Torah.

rashiTranslations.he: שבת איקרי. ובלאו ג"ש נמי נפקא מיניה וביה דכיון דאיקרי שבת
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi: the implication of the doubled 'Shabbat' title for the labor prohibition.

rashiTranslations.he: לענין עינוי הוה ליה כשבת לעונש ואזהרה: תשבתו שבתכם. ומשבת
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi: food not fit for eating does not count toward the YK eating measure.

rashiTranslations.he: שבתון לא גמר דאיקרי שבת דאיצטריך ליה לרבויי כל הענין
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi: bile and other unpalatable substances are excluded from the measure.

rashiTranslations.he: כדאמר בריש פרקין (דף עד.) שבתון
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi explains the opening discussion of this topic on 81b.

rashiTranslations.he: שבות אי נמי שבת שבתון היא לאו
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi explains the opening discussion of this topic on 81b.

rashiTranslations.he: יומא איקרי שבת אלא הכי קאמר
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi explains the opening discussion of this topic on 81b.

rashiTranslations.he: מנוח' מרגוע היא לכם והיכא נמי
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi explains the opening discussion of this topic on 81b.

rashiTranslations.he: דכתיב שבת שבתון הוא לשון זכר אף
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi explains the opening discussion of this topic on 81b.

rashiTranslations.he: לשון מנוחה הוא לשון זכר אבל יומא
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi elaborates on the halachic details of this sugya.

rashiTranslations.he: לא איקרי שבת להכי נקט תשבתו
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi elaborates on the halachic details of this sugya.

rashiTranslations.he: שבתכם יתירא דהוא גופיה איקרי
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi elaborates on the halachic details of this sugya.

rashiTranslations.he: שבת: מיבעיא ליה לכדתניא. לשאר
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi elaborates on the halachic details of this sugya.

rashiTranslations.he: ימים טובים ולא איום כפורים
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi elaborates on the halachic details of this sugya.

rashiTranslations.he: קאי: ותנא דעצם עצם. מדאיצטריך
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi elaborates on the halachic details of this sugya.

rashiTranslations.he: למעוטי לתוספת החול מעונש
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi elaborates on the halachic details of this sugya.

rashiTranslations.he: ומאזהרה ממילא שמעינן דמוסיפין
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi elaborates on the halachic details of this sugya.

rashiTranslations.he: והאי ועניתם בתשעה מאי עביד ליה:
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi elaborates on the halachic details of this sugya.

rashiTranslations.he: כל האוכל ושותה וכו'. והכי משמע
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi elaborates on the halachic details of this sugya.

rashiTranslations.he: קרא ועניתם בתשעה כלומר התקן
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: עצמך בתשעה שתוכל להתענות
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: בעשרה ומדאפקיה קרא בלשון עינוי
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: לומר לך הרי הוא כאלו מתענה
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: בתשעה: כס פלפלין. אין זה יישוב
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: הדעת שאין זה דרך אכילתו: מיתיבי
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: וכו'. אלמא מאכל קרי ליה: שהפלפלין
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: חייבין בערלה. מין אילן הוא: רטיבא.
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: חזי למיכל: הימלתא. ליטוריג"ה
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: שמפטמים בשמים כתושים בדבש: מבי
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: הנדואי. מארץ כוש: שריא. ואין בו
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi glosses 'ha-Indavi' (the Indaean spice): it comes from the land of Kush. 'Permitted' - and it does not have...

rashiTranslations.he: משום בישולי נכרים שנאכל כמות
rashiTranslations.en: ...the prohibition of bishul akum (Gentile cooking), since it is eaten...

rashiTranslations.he: שהוא חי ולא משום גיעולי נכרים
rashiTranslations.en: ...raw; and it also lacks the prohibition of geilui akum (absorbed Gentile flavor)...

rashiTranslations.he: דנותן טעם לפגם ומדקאמר אין בו
rashiTranslations.en: ...because it only imparts a deteriorated taste. From the Tanna's ruling that there is no prohibition of bishul akum, we derive...

rashiTranslations.he: משום בישולי נכרים ש"מ דרך אכילתו
rashiTranslations.en: ...that its normal manner of eating is raw.

rashiTranslations.he: כשהוא חי: מר"ה עד יום הכפורים.
rashiTranslations.en: When raw. Rashi then notes the phrase 'from Rosh Hashanah until Yom Kippur.'

rashiTranslations.he: אבל לבלבו קודם ר"ה כבר נעשו
rashiTranslations.en: But if the buds sprouted before Rosh Hashanah, they have already become...

rashiTranslations.he: קשים ועץ בעלמא נינהו: משיב
rashiTranslations.en: ...hard, and they are mere wood (not a food item).

rashiTranslations.he: את הנפש. ומבטל את העינוי:
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi on 'restores the soul' (mishiv et ha-nefesh): it nullifies the affliction of the Yom Kippur fast.

rashiTranslations.he: מזגו. נתנו בו מים להתיש כחו:
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi glosses 'diluted it' (mezago): they added water to it to weaken its potency.

rashiTranslations.he: מתני'
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi opens the Mishnah section heading, marking the next passage.
